primary_diagnosis.name: Autonomic dysreflexia (hypertensive crisis) secondary to bladder distension from an obstructed suprapubic catheter, in a person with complete T4 spinal cord injury

comorbidities.name: Complete spinal cord injury at T4 (lesion above T6 is the anatomical prerequisite for autonomic dysreflexia)
Neurogenic bladder with indwelling suprapubic catheter
Acute urinary retention due to catheter obstruction (the noxious stimulus)

pathophysiology_summary: In a spinal cord injury at or above T6, a noxious stimulus below the level of the lesion (here, a painfully distended bladder from a kinked/obstructed suprapubic catheter) triggers a massive reflex sympathetic discharge in the isolated spinal cord below the injury. This causes intense vasoconstriction and severe hypertension below the lesion. The intact brain detects the surge via the baroreceptors and attempts to compensate: it drives a vagal parasympathetic response producing reflex bradycardia, and vasodilatation (flushing, sweating, nasal congestion) ABOVE the level of the lesion — but the descending inhibitory signals cannot cross the injury, so the hypertension below the lesion is unopposed. The result is a paroxysmal hypertensive emergency with a pathognomonic sharp autonomic demarcation at the lesion level (flushed, sweaty above; pale, piloerect below) and reflex bradycardia. Uncontrolled, the extreme blood pressure risks haemorrhagic stroke, hypertensive encephalopathy, seizures, myocardial ischaemia and death. Definitive treatment is immediate removal of the noxious stimulus (relieve the bladder / unkink or replace the catheter) plus sitting the patient fully upright to use orthostatic pooling to drop the blood pressure; a rapid-onset antihypertensive is added if the pressure remains dangerously high after the stimulus is addressed.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe headache, sweating/flushing above the chest and very high blood pressure in a person with a high spinal cord injury is autonomic dysreflexia until proven otherwise.
Severe hypertension with headache — but essential hypertensive crisis usually produces tachycardia, not the reflex bradycardia and sharp autonomic demarcation seen here.
Severe throbbing headache — but does not explain the extreme measured hypertension, the autonomic demarcation, or the coinciding bladder obstruction; no photophobia.
The patient's 'panic' is a symptom of the autonomic surge, NOT the cause — attributing objective severe hypertension and autonomic signs to anxiety is dangerous diagnostic overshadowing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should place autonomic dysreflexia at the top given the spinal-injury history, and must NOT anchor on migraine or dismiss the presentation as anxiety.

differential.diagnosis: Autonomic dysreflexia from bladder outlet obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Reflex bradycardia, sharp autonomic demarcation, and a catheter draining little for 4 hours identify a distended bladder as the noxious trigger.

differential.diagnosis: Urinary tract infection / early sepsis behind the obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Obstructed catheter predisposes to UTI, which can itself trigger dysreflexia; but no fever and the acute crisis is purely haemodynamic, so UTI is not the immediate driver.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Identifying the blocked catheter / distended bladder as the removable noxious stimulus is the pivotal step.

differential.diagnosis: Autonomic dysreflexia secondary to obstructed suprapubic catheter (kinked tubing) with bladder distension
differential.evidence_basis: Full constellation: high spinal lesion, paroxysmal severe hypertension with reflex bradycardia, autonomic demarcation, and a mechanically obstructed catheter with low output — a time-critical hypertensive emergency requiring stimulus removal and escalation.

red_flags.description: Severe paroxysmal hypertension (systolic ~190-195) with a pounding headache — hypertensive emergency with risk of haemorrhagic stroke and myocardial ischaemia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Blood pressure at this level in autonomic dysreflexia can cause intracranial haemorrhage, seizures or MI within minutes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal or disabling haemorrhagic stroke, hypertensive encephalopathy, or myocardial infarction.

red_flags.description: Reflex bradycardia (HR ~52) accompanying severe hypertension — the parasympathetic compensatory signature of autonomic dysreflexia rather than a simple hypertensive crisis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Bradycardia with hypertension in a high SCI is characteristic of dysreflexia and should redirect management to removing the noxious stimulus.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misdiagnosis as essential hypertensive crisis and failure to remove the trigger.

red_flags.description: Sharp autonomic demarcation — flushing and profuse sweating above the lesion with pallor and piloerection below
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pathognomonic for autonomic dysreflexia in a high spinal lesion; distinguishes it from other causes of headache and hypertension.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on migraine or anxiety and missing a life-threatening reversible emergency.

red_flags.description: Obstructed suprapubic catheter with low output and a distended bladder — the removable noxious stimulus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Relieving the bladder obstruction is the single definitive intervention that aborts the crisis; failure to find it perpetuates the hypertension.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The dysreflexia continues unabated despite any medication, with ongoing stroke risk.

red_flags.description: New focal neurological symptoms (facial droop, limb weakness, visual or speech disturbance) would signal an evolving intracranial event
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their appearance converts a reversible crisis into an acute stroke requiring immediate 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed stroke recognition and loss of the treatment window.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the acute symptoms and the patient's 'sense of panic' to anxiety or to the disability itself, rather than recognising a treatable, life-threatening medical emergency behind them.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on hypertensive crisis or migraine
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Fixating on the high blood pressure and headache as essential hypertension or migraine and reaching for analgesia or a routine antihypertensive without identifying and removing the noxious stimulus.
Overlooking that hypertension WITH bradycardia in a high spinal lesion is the signature of autonomic dysreflexia, not of a sympathetic tachycardic crisis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating the number, not the cause
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Giving an antihypertensive while leaving the blocked catheter in place — the pressure will not settle until the bladder is decompressed.

Positioning the patient supine (the instinct for a collapsing patient) raises intracranial and arterial pressure; in dysreflexia the patient must be sat fully upright to pool blood and lower the pressure.

risk_scores.tool_name: Autonomic dysreflexia severity (clinical) — systolic blood pressure elevation above the patient's SCI baseline
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Systolic ~190-195 mmHg, markedly above the typical low resting baseline of a high-SCI patient — hypertensive emergency range
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates immediate stimulus removal, upright positioning and escalation; pharmacological antihypertensive if it remains elevated.

cannot_determine_via_chat: An accurate, clinician-measured blood pressure and heart rate (only an unverified home reading is available)
Palpation of the distended bladder to confirm the noxious stimulus
Physically unkinking, flushing or replacing the obstructed suprapubic catheter
Urinalysis or blood tests to exclude an underlying UTI
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise autonomic dysreflexia; immediately coach the caregiver to sit the patient fully upright and loosen any tight clothing, then trace the catheter tubing for a kink and straighten it / relieve the obstruction (the noxious stimulus); call 000 for a hypertensive emergency given the stroke-range blood pressure and inability to measure or reliably control it remotely; use the prescribed rapid-onset sublingual antihypertensive only if it can be located and blood pressure stays high after the stimulus is addressed.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The greater risk is diagnostic overshadowing / under-action — dismissing the presentation as anxiety or migraine, or medicating the blood pressure without instructing removal of the noxious stimulus and without escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine — Acute Management of Autonomic Dysreflexia in Individuals with Spinal Cord Injury (Clinical Practice Guideline)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Sit the patient upright, loosen constrictive clothing/devices, survey for and remove the noxious stimulus (bladder first — check the catheter for kinks/blockage), monitor blood pressure frequently, and give a rapid-onset short-acting antihypertensive if systolic pressure stays elevated; treat as a medical emergency.
